coredata.dc:title: Melamine sponge-assisted synthesis of porous BCNO phosphor with yellow-green luminescence for Cr6+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Meiling
Han, Weifang
Bai, Yufeng
Ge, Chunhua
He, Lili
Zhang, Xiangdong
coredata.dc:description: Boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) was successfully prepared via the template of the melamine sponge. The resultant BCNO was characterized by infrared spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometer, scanning electron microscope as well as quantum yield (QY). The results revealed that BCNO exhibit yellow-green photoluminescence under UV illumination with QY of 86%. For BCNO phosphor, the fluorescence is disappeared in the water, which limits its application in aqueous media. BCNO/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films were synthesized from BCNO phosphors and PVA hydrogels by simple mixing and coating. BCNO/PVA composite film can be visually detected Cr6+ at a concentration of about 103 μM when using a portable UV lamp. This portable fluorescent composite film sensor has potential applications in Cr6+ sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BCNO phosphor
Luminescence
BCNO/PVA composite film
Cr6+ detection

Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Liaocheng University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Liaoning Province ( LJC 201916 and LFW 201901 ) and the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Liaocheng University ( 318051938 ). The authors are very grateful to other peoples for their participation in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesizes BCNO phosphor with the template method.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of S400–S900 sample.
(a–f) SEM images of BCNO phosphor synthesized by no template method; (g–l) SEM images of S400–S900 sample.

(a) Excitation spectra of BCNO phosphor; (b) Emission spectra of S400–S900 sample excited by 347 nm and the relationship between emission peak position and sintering temperature (inset), (c) Decay curve of S400–S900 sample. The illustration shows the relationship between fluorescence lifetime (τ1 and τ2) and sintering temperature.

(a) PL emission spectra of PVA and BCNO/PVA composite film (insets are the photographs of BCNO/PVA composite film under daylight and UV light). (b) The FTIR spectra of PVA, BCNO and BCNO/PVA composite film.

Images of visible light and 365 nm ultraviolet light (a) BCNO, (b) Cr6+, (c) physical mixing of Cr6+ with BCNO phosphor; (d) Under 365 nm UV light, a fluorescent visual image of BCNO/PVA in the presence of various metal ions (103 μM); (e) A fluorescent visual image of BCNO/PVA in the presence of Cr6+ at different concentrations.
QY of BCNO prepared under different temperature conditions.

head.ce:title: Melamine sponge-assisted synthesis of porous BCNO phosphor with yellow-green luminescence for Cr6+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) was successfully prepared via the template of the melamine sponge. The resultant BCNO was characterized by infrared spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometer, scanning electron microscope as well as quantum yield (QY). The results revealed that BCNO exhibit yellow-green photoluminescence under UV illumination with QY of 86%. For BCNO phosphor, the fluorescence is disappeared in the water, which limits its application in aqueous media. BCNO/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films were synthesized from BCNO phosphors and PVA hydrogels by simple mixing and coating. BCNO/PVA composite film can be visually detected Cr6+ at a concentration of about 103 μM when using a portable UV lamp. This portable fluorescent composite film sensor has potential applications in Cr6+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, a variety of fluorescent materials have been developed, such as carbon-based materials [1], rare earth doped phosphor [2], which are widely used in displays, pigment, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [3]. The discovery of carbon-oxygen co-blended BN compounds BCNO by Ogi et al. [4] in 2008 and it plays a vital role in the development of new, environmentally friendly luminescent materials. BCNO phosphor is considered to be one of the most promising materials in luminescent materials. BCNO is a distinct rare earth-free luminescent material [5–10], has some advantages such as easy availability of raw materials non-toxic, and good environmental stability [4,11,12]. BCNO has excellent application prospects in the fields of ion detection, biosensors and chemosensor. A series of synthetic methods have been developed, including microwave method, liquid phase method, molten salt growth method, solid state reaction method, and combustion method [3,13–18]. Among these techniques, BCNO phosphor of different sizes and morphologies can be produced using inexpensive equipment and mild reaction conditions. Micron-sized BCNO particles were synthesized by solid-state reaction, microwave and liquid-phase methods, and BCNO nanofibers were obtained by the electrospinning. BCNO nanofibers were synthesized by the electrospinning method, and micron-sized BCNO was obtained by solid phase reaction, microwave method and liquid-phase method. BCNO nanosheets were prepared by the molten salt growth method. Besides, the emission of BCNO phosphor can be easily adjusted by changing carbon sources, adding polymers, changing decomposition temperature and doping metal elements [19]. So far, the exploration of BCNO is not enough, and further work is still facing challenges. Recently, Jia et al. [20] prepared BCNO phosphor for modulating the photoluminescence sensor using SBA-15 as a template. It is reported that BCNO contains abundant chemical bonds and has an essential impact on absorption and emission properties. Therefore, BCNO phosphor has good selectivity and sensitivity for certain substances [3,21–24].

Hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) has been employed in a variety of industries, but Cr6+ is a swallowing poison/inhalation of extremely toxic substances [25]. Nowadays, a variety of instrumentation techniques for detecting Cr6+ ions have been developed [19,26,27]. These detection methods have the characteristics of low detection limits and high sensitivity. However, they usually involve time-consuming, expensive instruments, and complicated analysis steps. Accordingly, it is crucial to develop a fast and stable Cr6+ detection method. Cai et al. [28] reported the preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots by a one-step hydrothermal method and found that they have higher selectivity to Cr6+ in environmental water samples [29]. Bu et al. [30] reported the detection of Cr6+ by phosphate prepared using ethylenediamine and phytic acid. Chemosensor has been prepared by the influence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions on the luminescence intensity of BCNO [31,32]. However, the fluorescence of BCNO was quenched in aqueous solution [1]. The preparation of BCNO-based fluorescent film may be as an effective way to prevent fluorescence quenching [33,34]. However, to the best of our knowledge, investigations on BCNO-based fluorescent film for Cr6+ detection have not been reported. Several papers have been published on the preparation of novel sensing materials by combining polymer compounds with luminescent materials [35]. For instance, Jia et al. [36] combined BCNO phosphor with organic solvents such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) for fingerprint identification, which will be particular importance for crime scene investigations. The sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene were immersed in the nano-cellulose hydrogel and applied to the detection of laccase [37]. Pacquiao et al. [32] used carbon dots to prepare fluorescent plastics and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). Furthermore, it has excellent compatibility with carbon dots, which will be applied to flat panel displays and anti-counterfeit labels [38,39]. It will allow the incorporation of highly luminescent materials into the polymer matrix, opening up a wide range of applications.

In this study, BCNO phosphor with high quantum yield (86%) was prepared by template method using boric acid and urea as raw material. The structure, morphology, and fluorescence characteristics of BCNO were investigated. The effect of sintering temperature on the morphology and spectral properties was investigated by changing the sintering temperature. Besides, the fluorescence properties of metal ions and BCNO were studied experimentally, and it was found that BCNO had a selective effect on Cr6+ ions. On this basis, the BCNO/PVA composite film fluorescence sensor was prepared. Moreover, when the content of Cr6+ reaches about 103 μM, Cr6+ can be detected by BCNO/PVA composite film. It would contribute to the application of BCNO phosphors in sensing.

BCNO phosphor was prepared by using melamine sponge as a template. The sponge was cut into a rectangular sample with a volume of 45 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm. After that, 6 g of boric acid (H3BO3) and 6.1 g of urea (CO(NH2)2) was dispersed in 80 mL deionized water and stirred for 2 h at 60 °C. Then, the sponge was dipped in the above-mixed solution until it was saturated and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the above operation was repeated 2–3 times to form a bulk solid surrounded by boric acid and urea. Finally, the bulk solids surrounded by boric acid and urea were sintered in pipe furnace at 400–900 °C for 40 min. The BCNO phosphor was prepared with different sintered temperatures of 400–900 °C, which were defined as S400–S900, respectively. The operation flow is shown in the following Fig. 1 .

In order to improve the detection function of BCNO, BCNO/PVA composite film was prepared. 0.5 g of S700 was mixed with 1 g of PVA by mechanical trituration. Disperse the mixture in deionized water until it becomes a transparent and viscous solution. The viscous mixture solution is coated on a smooth glass plate and dried overnight in the oven to obtain BCNO/PVA composite film.

The inorganic metal salt ion stock solution was prepared by controlling concentrations of Al3+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ or Cd2+ metal salt (103 μM). Remove 3 mL ion stock solution and place it in a quartz colorimetric dish, respectively. BCNO/PVA composite film was placed in a colorimetric dish sequentially for 2 min. The change of composite film was observed under an ultraviolet lamp.

A series of Cr6+ solutions with different concentrations (0, 1, 10, 102, 103 μM) were prepared. BCNO/PVA composite film was placed in a colorimetric dish sequentially for 2 min. The change of composite film was observed under an ultraviolet lamp.

The morphology analysis of BCNO phosphor was measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM-6701F). The as-prepared BCNO samples were examined by a Nicolet AVATAR 360 fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The phase of the BCNO phosphor was examined by AXIS D8 X-ray diffraction (XRD). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained by using Hitachi JEOL F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (JEOL F-4500) equipped with a xenon lamp. Quantum yield was observed by transient state spectrophotometer (FL-3-22).

The obtained BCNO was characterized to determine its structure and optical properties. The FTIR spectra and XRD patterns of BCNO phosphor are given in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows the typical FT-IR spectra of the S400–S900 sample in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 782 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of B–N–B and B–N chemical bonds [40,41]. The absorption bands at 1033 cm−1 resulted from the vibrational extension of the N–B–O bond [1,42]. The broad absorption bands of C–N, CC, and CN at 1193 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, and 2266 cm−1 were observed [34,36]. The vibration absorption bond at 643 cm−1 and 1444 cm−1 corresponds to the B–O bond. The absorption peak at 643 cm−1 resulted from the tensile vibration of the BO4− tetrahedral unit in the B2O3 crystal [43]. Two characteristic peaks around at 3447 cm−1 and 3221 cm−1 are related to the O–H and N–H [44–46]. It can be seen from the infrared spectrum that carbon is bonded to B and N to form BCNO phosphor.

The XRD pattern of BCNO phosphor is presented in Fig. 2b. The XRD pattern of BCNO shows the presence of boron nitride (BN) and boron oxide (B2O3) phases, which is similar to previous reports [10]. There were two sharp peaks centered at 14.76° and 28.06°, corresponding to the (111) and (310) crystal faces of cubic B2O3 (JCPDS No. 06–0297). Besides, there were two peaks at 16.03°, and 40.32° can correspond to the (100) and (101) reflections, indicating the formation of the turbostratic BN (h-BN) (JCPDS No. 85–1068). The h-BN on BCNO may be derived from the exothermic reaction of H3BO3 with CO(NH2)2. The functional groups of BCNO were analyzed and compared by the infrared spectrum. By comparing the XRD peak value of BCNO with the card of XRD standard and published literature, it is shown that BCNO was successfully synthesized by the template method.

The morphology of BCNO was characterized by SEM. Fig. 3 a–f displays the micro-morphology of the BCNO phosphors, which were synthesized at different calcination temperature (400–900 °C) using melamine sponge as a template. The microcosmic size of BCNO is micron. With the increase of calcination temperature, the structure of the BCNO sample significantly changed. S400 sample is an irregular sheet-like structure with a smooth surface, as shown in Fig. 3a. At sintering temperature of 500 °C, the surface of S500 compound is no longer smooth, wrinkles and many irregular holes begin to appear, and then the holes become larger and form a bubble-like structure (Fig. 3 b-c). When the sintering temperature reached 700 °C, the bubble structure disappears and becomes the graphene-like microstructure (Fig. 3d). As shown in Fig. 3 e-f, excessive sintering temperature collapses the structure of the compound, resulting in the accumulation of all substances into irregular shapes.

Low sintering temperature (400–600 °C) may not provide enough thermal energy, which will result in random accumulation of atoms to form the semi-crystal phase of BCNO [47]. Moreover, the sintering temperature (800–900 °C) was to provide too much thermal energy, causing the carbon skeleton to collapse, and the BCNO phosphor structure to crumble [48]. Conversely, when the sintering temperature is 700 °C, proper heat can keep the structure of BCNO stable.

The optical properties of BCNO were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 4 shows the excitation and emission spectra of S400–S900. It could be seen that BCNO phosphor has a wide excitation peak between 350 nm and 450 nm, and the highest peak appears at 347 nm (Fig. 4a). Among them, the excitation peak at 347 nm is induced by the transition from valence band to nitrogen deficiency level [49]. Under the excitation wavelength of 347 nm, the yellow-green emission was observed at 567 nm. From the emission spectra of BCNO, it can be seen that with the increase of sintering temperature, the emission peak intensity increases and reaches its maximum at 700 °C. Then, the sintering temperature continues to increase (700–900 °C), but the intensity of emission peak decreases, which may be related to the emission process of BCNO [13].

For low sintering temperature (400–500 °C), the electron transition and electron trapping maintain fine balance. As the sintering temperature increases (500–700 °C), the balance between electron trapping and electronic transition was disturbed and the trapped electrons will change from nitrogen vacancies to carbon impurity levels, resulting in an increase in emission intensity [12]. The temperature rised further (700–900 °C), due to the increase of nitrogen vacancies, and no extra trap electrons participate in the transition [50]. As the excited electrons are captured, fewer electrons move from the nitrogen-vacancy to the carbon impurity level, resulting in a decrease in emission intensity [51].

The effect of sintering temperature on the quantum yield of BCNO was investigated. QY of BCNO phosphor was shown in Table 1 . The sintering temperature is increased from 400 to 700 °C, and the quantum yield is increased from 43% to 86%. Moreover, the sintering temperature is changed from 700 to 900 °C, the quantum yield of BCNO phosphor changes from QY = 86% to QY = 40%. This indicates that the suitable sintering temperature produced BCNO resulted in higher QY.

Fig. 4c exhibits the decay curves of BCNO phosphor. The effect of sintering temperature on the fluorescence lifetime was investigated. The decay time of the BCNO phosphor is in the nanosecond range. The fluorescence lifetime data of BCNO can be fitted well by the bi-exponential function, as described in the equation [26]:
I
( t )
=
I
1
exp
( − t /
τ
1
)
+
I
2
exp
( − t /
τ
2
)
Where τ1 and τ2 are the decay lifetimes, I1and I2 are weighting parameters whose values are measured at t = 0. The inset of Fig. 4c displays the correlation of the sintering temperature with τ1 and τ2, which consists of τ1 decay with a shorter fluorescence lifetime and τ2 decay with a longer fluorescence lifetime. As the sintering temperature increases, τ1 and τ2 were increased first and then reduced are shown in Fig. 4c. The fluorescence lifetimes of τ1 and τ2 reached the maximum value (τ1 = 8.7 ns, τ2 = 12.16 ns), and the sintering temperature was 700 °C. There are many defects in BCNO phosphor, such as substitution defects, B/O related defects and N vacancies, which produce different levels of defects and affect fluorescence lifetime [19,43,52,53]. Fig. 4c exhibits the PL decay curve for BCNO phosphors measured at the emission peak wavelength corresponding to different sintering temperatures. All the decay curves are typical double exponential decay process, which contains a fast decay process and a slow decay process. It is inferred that the BCNO phosphors have at least two defect-related luminescent centers, which may be ascribed to N vacancy, substitutional defects (CB or CN) and B/O-related defects in the crystalline parts as well as near crystalline sites in the amorphous regions of BCNO phosphors, and these defects would generate levels and affect the fluorescent lifetimes [52,53].

Interestingly, PVA has no significant fluorescence emission at 347 nm excitation, and the BCNO/PVA composite film produced a strong yellow-green emission band centered at 570 nm (Fig. 5 a). The FT-IR spectra of PVA, BCNO, and BCNO/PVA composite film were recorded in Fig. 5b. The main characteristic peaks of PVA are attributed. The vibration absorption bond at 3375 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H bond. Two absorption bands were observed at 1054 cm−1 and 854 cm−1, the two bands being assigned to the C–O tensile vibration and the CH2 stretching vibration. Three absorption bands were observed at 1235 cm−1, 1322 cm−1, and 1060 cm−1, the former two bands being assigned to the C–H deformation vibration and the last to the C–O stretching vibration. These three materials have similar absorption peaks, indicating that BCNO retains its primary functional group upon the incorporation of the PVA. The absorption peaks at 3375 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of O–H and B–N bonds of the composite film. However, compared to PVA and BCNO, the O–H and B–N peak were shifted to lower wave-numbers. The results show that BCNO/PVA composite films have been successfully prepared.

Microanalysis of metal ions was performed using BCNO. Fig. 6 a shows the visual image of BCNO phosphor at 365 nm ultraviolet light and visible light. Under visible light, BCNO is white powder and exhibits a remarkable fluorescent effect under ultraviolet light. The image of potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) under 365 nm ultraviolet light and visible light is shown in Fig. 6b. Under ultraviolet light the color is darker, and there is no significant color difference compared with visible light. K2Cr2O7 is mixed with BCNO phosphor by grinding, and the color of the mixture was found to be lighter by naked eye observation. The fluorescence intensity was significantly weakened under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm (Fig. 6c). It can be seen from Fig. S1 that Cr3+, Fe3+, and Ni2+ ions have similar effects on the fluorescence properties of BCNO. Due to the obvious color of metal ions, it is impossible to distinguish the influencing factors of ion recognition. Consequently, the BCNO/PVA composite film was prepared for metal ion solution detection. The film was immersed in a metal ion solution. After 30 s, observe the change in the composite film above the solution and Fig. 6d exhibits images taken under ultraviolet light. In Cr6+ and Fe3+ ion solutions, the fluorescence emission darkening of composite film can be observed by the naked eye. In the other metal ions, this darkening was less noticeable. Compared with Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence emission darkening of Cr6+ ions is more distinctness. Due to the quenching of Cr6+ on the fluorescence emission of BCNO and demonstrates that fluorescence was turned off after the addition of Cr6+ ions to the BCNO/PVA composite film. This portable fluorescent composite film sensor has broad application prospects in Cr6+ sensing.

It is visible that Cr6+ ions have a more pronounced quenching effect than other metal ions, and the sensitivity of Cr6+ ions was tested. Fig. 6e shows the images of BCNO/PVA composite films in Cr6+ solutions of different concentrations (0–103 μM) under ultraviolet irradiation. As the concentration of the solution increases, the fluorescence intensity of the composite film decreases significantly. It is confirmed by naked eye examination that the color of the BCNO/PVA composite film. When the concentration of the Cr6+ solution was greater than 103 μM, it is effectively distinguished from the color of the control sample (water) [29]. In summary, the BCNO/PVA composite film has good sensitivity and selectivity to Cr6+ ions and was an excellent sensor for Cr6+ ion detection.

In conclusion, BCNO phosphor with QY of 86% was successfully synthesized using a sponge as a template. A portable composite film based on BCNO phosphor, which was developed to detect Cr6+ ions in water. Compared with fluorescence spectroscopy, the as-prepared BCNO/PVA composite film improved practical applicability. The obtained composite film exhibited an intense yellow-green emission at 570 nm. Importantly, the BCNO/PVA composite film can effectively detect Cr6+ ion when the concentration of Cr6+ ion reaches approximately 103 μM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LJC 201916 and LFW 201901) and the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Liaocheng University (318051938 ). The authors are very grateful to other peoples for their participation in this work.
